channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Iran Approach Sows Confusion
item.description: President Trump’s pendulum swings on Iran have often seemed driven by mood and moment rather than any discernible strategy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s shifts reflect a political tug of war between hawkish supporters urging him to hit Iran harder and noninterventionists urging him to make a quick deal.

item.title: A Draft U.S.-Iran Plan Is Said to Be on the Table. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: U.S. and Iranian officials say they are closing in on the terms of a preliminary agreement. Yet sticking points, particularly over the Strait of Hormuz, remain.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran depicting the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in May.

item.title: In Carroll Lawsuits Inquiry, Scrutiny Turns Toward Private Citizens Who Antagonized Trump
item.description: The Justice Department is said to be examining the funding of lawsuits brought by E. Jean Carroll, an author who has never sought a public role, political power or governmental authority.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll, the author and columnist, leaving court in Manhattan in 2024 after a hearing on one of her lawsuits against Donald J. Trump.

item.title: Where Do E. Jean Carroll’s Lawsuits Against President Trump Stand?
item.description: Two monetary judgments against Mr. Trump are winding their way through the legal system, with the Supreme Court likely to be the final word on both.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll leaves court after closing arguments in her defamation trial against Donald Trump in 2023.

item.title: Blue Origin Rocket Blows Up on Florida Launchpad During Test
item.description: The rocket, built by the Jeff Bezos-owned space company, was to carry 48 satellites into space. Blue Origin reported on social media that “all personnel have been accounted for.”
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Malcolm Denemark/USA Today, via Imagn Images, via Reuters
item.media:description: The Blue Origin launchpad seen in flames from Cocoa Beach, Fla. on Thursday night.

item.title: Scripps Spelling Bee 2026 Takeaways: Shrey Parikh Wins in Spell-Off
item.description: The competition came down to a spell-off. Contestants were eliminated early, and a sixth grader became a crowd favorite.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shrey Parikh, a 14-year-old from Rancho Cucamonga, Calif., broke the record for the most words spelled correctly in a spell-off.

item.title: Not Every Language Has Spelling Bees. Here’s Why English Is Perfect for Them.
item.description: English’s hybrid history, silent letters and overcomplicated rules are what make the competition fierce.
item.dc:creator: Nitsuh Abebe

item.title: In Argentina, U.S. Tech Billionaire Peter Thiel Finds An Escape
item.description: The billionaire’s new roots in Argentina are said to be partly motivated by concerns about the future of the United States and shared beliefs with Argentina’s right-wing leader.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Matias Baglietto/Reuters
item.media:description: Peter Thiel, right, arriving for a meeting with President Javier Milei at the Casa Rosada Presidential Palace in Buenos Aires in April.

item.title: Europe Is Edging Closer to a Trade War With China. Here’s Why.
item.description: As cheap goods pour in, threatening the continent’s manufacturing sector, a search for solutions is becoming increasingly urgent.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Chinese-made electric vehicles awaiting export at the port in Hangzhou, eastern China, in May.

item.title: Russian Drone Hits Romanian Apartment Building, Officials Say
item.description: NATO condemned “Russia’s recklessness” and confirmed that it was in touch with the Romanian authorities.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Inquam Photos, via Reuters
item.media:description: The site where a drone hit a building in Galati, Romania, on Friday, close to the border with Ukraine.

item.title: ‘Both Parties Kind of Get It Wrong’: The Young Men Who May Swing the Midterms
item.description: Many Gen Z men who voted for Donald Trump are dismayed by his time in office. But they say they are not hearing an appealing pitch from Democrats, either.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times

item.title: Swallowing Concerns, G.O.P. Senators Rally Behind Paxton
item.description: After attacking the Texas attorney general, who won the G.O.P. nomination, Senate Republicans are pivoting sharply to support him in what is now a competitive race.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, beat Senator John Cornyn by close to 30 points on Tuesday night, transforming Texas into a battleground state.

item.title: A Democrat’s Dodge on AIPAC Points to the Party’s Tensions Over Israel
item.description: A key moment at a Senate primary debate in Michigan showed how pro-Israel Democrats are approaching the issue with caution.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Danielle James/The Flint Journal, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mallory McMorrow, Haley Stevens and Dr. Abdul El-Sayed are locked in a tight Democratic primary race for Senate in Michigan.

item.title: Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer Will Not Run for President in 2028
item.description: The Michigan governor made the remarks to a reporter on Thursday morning. Later, she sounded less definitive. Ms. Whitmer has been viewed as part of a field of possible Democratic candidates.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer of Michigan at an event in Detroit in April.

item.title: Treasury Prepares to Make Trump the Face of a New $250 Bill
item.description: Treasury Secretary Bessent backed the idea, which would require legislation allowing a living person to appear on currency.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he supported the idea of putting President Trump’s portrait on a new $250 bill.

item.title: Iran, Gas Prices, the Ballroom: Trump Professes Indifference Over Fallout
item.description: President Trump has increasingly adopted a posture of nonchalance when pushed on the political consequences of his actions.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: “I don’t care about the midterms,” President Trump said at a cabinet meeting on Wednesday.

item.title: After New Push by the Bolsonaros, U.S. Labels Brazilian Gangs as Terrorist Groups
item.description: Following through on threats, the Trump administration has designated Brazil’s two largest drug gangs as terrorist groups.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:credit: Mauro Pimentel/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Red Command being arrested last year in Rio de Janeiro. The U.S. designated the gang a terrorist group on Thursday.

item.title: Suit Says Black Infants Were Subjected to Experimental Vaccine Without Consent
item.description: The babies were part of a vaccine trial for a respiratory virus in the 1960s and died shortly after. Their families said they had been unaware of the trial until recently.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Benjamin Crump is representing the families of two Black infants who were unknowingly enrolled as test subjects in a mid-1960s vaccine trial for R.S.V.

item.title: The World’s 50 Best Restaurants Announces Its 2026 North America List
item.description: At a ceremony in New Orleans on Thursday, a Midwestern fine-dining standout took the top spot.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin

item.title: After an Explosion at a Washington Paper Mill, Mourning Mixes With Dread
item.description: The disaster at the mill in Longview, Wash., which took at least eight lives, was greeted with sadness but not surprise by a community that knew cutbacks and other pressures had raised risks.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Aaron West
item.media:credit: Mathieu Lewis-Rolland/Getty Images
item.media:description: A vigil on Tuesday for the victims of a fatal chemical storage tank failure in Longview, Wash.

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Leaves Them Laughing
item.description: In an interview, Jean Smart and Hannah Einbinder discuss the series finale, their offscreen relationship and why comedy is just like sex.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Chantal Anderson
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: Curating in the Cross Hairs: Is This the Smithsonian Chief’s Last Show?
item.description: Lonnie G. Bunch III, the Smithsonian secretary under pressure from the White House, organized an exhibit exploring America’s founding ideals.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I love thinking creatively about, how do you protect the Smithsonian?” said Lonnie G. Bunch III, the institution’s secretary. “How do you make sure issues of scholarship and integrity are there?”

item.title: With Pitchford v. Cain, The Supreme Court Protected Jury Rights. Now It Must Go Further.
item.description: All Americans benefit from the outcome of this case. And yet the decision highlights just how much work still needs to be done.
item.dc:creator: Avital Fried
item.media:credit: Aleksey Kondratyev for The New York Times

item.title: Hungary Showed How to Defeat an Autocrat
item.description: Hungary did it. The United States can, too.
item.dc:creator: M. Gessen and Máté Bartha
item.media:credit: Máté Bartha for The New York Times

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Finds Glee in President Trump’s Approval Rating
item.description: A new poll shows it sitting at 34 percent. “He has the same approval rating as ‘Paul Blart: Mall Cop,’” Kimmel said on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: Scooter Rider and Cyclist Are Killed in Head-On Crash on N.Y.C. Bridge
item.description: The collision on the Queensboro Bridge involved an e-scooter that is not legally allowed to be used on the streets of New York.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen and Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Christopher Maag/The New York Times
item.media:description: A bike was snapped in half after a collision with a scooter on the Queensboro Bridge.

item.title: Alaska Authorities Race to Rescue 4 Climbers Who Fell on Mount McKinley
item.description: The condition of the climbers was not immediately known.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Becky Bohrer/Associated Press
item.media:description: Weather conditions on the mountain were improving enough to allow for helicopter operations, officials said.

item.title: Italian Police Uncover Dead Mob Boss’s $230 Million Business Empire
item.description: When Matteo Messina Denaro died in 2023, many of his secrets died with him. That began to change with a tip about a wealthy Sicilian woman’s significant assets in a tiny European principality.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Carabinieri, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Matteo Messina Denaro being led from a police station after his arrest in Palermo in 2023.

item.title: Protesters Arrested at Immigration Detention Center as Tensions Rise
item.description: For nearly a week, relatives of migrants at Delaney Hall have said that some detainees have been on a hunger strike at the New Jersey facility.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley and Mark Bonamo
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki/Associated Press
item.media:description: Federal officials arrested demonstrators late Wednesday outside the Delaney Hall immigration detention center in Newark.
